content.text: Replacing an existing AI feature with a new agentic implementation is rarely a clean cutover. Production systems carry users, integrations, and SLA obligations that preclude a big-bang swap. The strangler pattern provides a migration path that wraps the legacy system in a routing facade, shifts traffic incrementally, and retires the old implementation only after the new one has proven itself in production.
What it is

The strangler pattern — named by Martin Fowler after the strangler fig tree that grows around and eventually replaces its host — is a migration strategy in which new functionality is introduced alongside the existing system rather than as a replacement of it. A routing facade sits in front of both systems and dispatches each request to either the legacy path or the new agentic path based on a routing criterion: a feature flag, a user cohort membership, a request type, or a percentage of traffic.

In AI migrations the pattern addresses a challenge specific to model-driven systems: the new agent's behavior cannot be fully validated in staging because real-user input distributions differ from synthetic test data. The strangler pattern enables production validation on real traffic, on a controlled subset, while the legacy system continues to serve the remaining traffic safely. The migration concludes only when the new agent's quality, reliability, and cost metrics meet the thresholds set for full rollout.

Applied to agentic AI migrations specifically, the strangler pattern typically spans three phases: (1) install the routing facade without shifting any traffic; (2) migrate user journeys or request types one at a time, expanding coverage as eval results confirm quality; and (3) retire the legacy system once it handles zero traffic and all associated infrastructure has been cleaned up.
Why it matters

AI feature migrations carry a risk profile unlike traditional software migrations. The new agent may produce correct outputs on average but fail on specific input distributions that were well-handled by the legacy system. A big-bang cutover would expose all users to those failures simultaneously. The strangler pattern limits the population exposed to any regression to the cohort currently assigned to the new agent, and it maintains the ability to shift traffic back to legacy within seconds if a quality problem is detected.

The pattern also provides a structured mechanism for running shadow mode comparisons. Before shifting any live traffic, the new agent can receive copies of production requests and produce outputs that are compared to the legacy system's responses — without those outputs ever reaching users. This shadow mode period is the most valuable phase of the migration because it exercises the new agent on real-world inputs with zero user-facing risk.

For teams that have an existing eval suite, the strangler pattern creates a natural quality gate at each migration increment: the eval suite must pass on the new agent for each request type before that type's traffic is migrated. This discipline prevents the accumulation of silent regressions that would otherwise be discovered only after full cutover.
Benefits
Zero-downtime migration: users on the legacy path experience no disruption while the new agent is being validated on the migrated path.
Real-traffic validation: shadow mode and canary testing expose the new agent to the actual input distribution before any user sees its outputs.
Granular rollback: if a regression is detected on the new agent, traffic can be shifted back to legacy for affected request types or cohorts without touching any other migration segments.
Incremental organizational learning: the team builds operational experience with the new agent on low-stakes user segments before it carries full traffic.
Parallel evolution: the legacy system can continue receiving bug fixes and minor improvements during the migration without blocking new agent development.
Clean decommission: the pattern defines a crisp ending condition — zero traffic on the legacy path — after which the legacy codebase can be deleted without uncertainty.
Trade-offs
Dual maintenance burden: both systems must be kept operational during the migration period; bugs that affect both paths must be fixed in both places.
Routing facade complexity: the facade must handle request routing, shadow mode traffic copying, and traffic percentage controls without introducing latency or becoming a new single point of failure.
Divergent behavior window: users on different paths may see different outputs for identical inputs, which is confusing if they share sessions or compare notes.
Shadow mode cost: running the new agent in shadow mode on production traffic doubles the LLM API call volume and cost during that phase.
Eval suite dependency: the migration quality gates only work if the eval suite covers the request types being migrated; poor eval coverage makes the gates unreliable.
How it works

The routing facade is the central component. It is a thin service or middleware layer positioned in front of both the legacy AI system and the new agent. The facade receives every incoming request and applies a routing decision function that returns one of three dispositions: LEGACY (send to legacy only), SHADOW (send to legacy as the live path, and also send a copy to the new agent for comparison), or NEW (send to new agent only).

During Phase 1, the routing function returns LEGACY for all requests. The new agent exists in the codebase and is deployed, but receives no traffic. The eval suite runs against the new agent in CI to establish a quality baseline. Shadow mode is activated for a small percentage of traffic (typically 5 percent) to gather the first real-world quality data.

During Phase 2, specific request types or user cohorts are migrated incrementally. For each increment, shadow mode results are reviewed, the eval suite is run on the migrated type, and only then is the routing function updated to return NEW for that segment. The legacy system's share of traffic shrinks with each increment. Traffic split percentages are controlled via feature flags that can be updated without a code deployment.

During Phase 3, the routing function returns NEW for all requests. The legacy system is decommissioned: its dependencies are removed, its infrastructure is torn down, and its codebase is deleted. The facade may be simplified or removed entirely once no routing decision is needed.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Strangler pattern three-phase migration: Phase 1 facade with all traffic to legacy, Phase 2 migration path by path, Phase 3 legacy deleted and new agent handles all traffic, with eval suite gate at every increment

content.text: The diagram shows the routing facade persisting across all three phases while the legacy system shrinks and the new agent grows. The eval suite bar at the bottom emphasizes that shadow mode comparison runs on every migration increment before traffic shifts. The green arrow from Phase 2 to Phase 3 represents the final migration gate after which legacy code is removed.
When to use it

Apply the strangler pattern when migrating from a rules-based or ML-model-based AI system to an agentic LLM-based system; when replacing one LLM agent with a substantially different one (different model, different tool set, different prompt architecture); or when migrating a stateful conversation system where session context must be handled consistently during the transition.

The pattern is most valuable when the system handles high traffic volumes, serves diverse user populations with varying input distributions, or has contractual SLAs that prohibit extended maintenance windows. For low-traffic internal tools or prototype systems with no SLA, a direct replacement (big-bang cutover with a rollback plan) may be more practical.

Do not use the strangler pattern for migrations that take longer than six months without reaching Phase 3. Extended dual-system maintenance is expensive and demoralizing. If the migration is projected to take longer, decompose it into smaller independently deliverable migrations each of which uses the strangler pattern.
How to implement
Design the routing facade as a stateless service with a pluggable routing function; the routing function should read its decisions from feature flags, not from hard-coded logic.
Instrument both the legacy path and the new agent path with identical observability: latency, error rate, cost per request, and eval score per request type.
Activate shadow mode for 5 to 10 percent of production traffic before migrating any live users; run the new agent's outputs through the eval suite and compare quality metrics to the legacy baseline.
Define acceptance criteria for each migration increment: minimum eval pass rate, maximum latency regression, and maximum cost per request increase.
Migrate the simplest, lowest-risk request types first to build confidence and catch integration issues before migrating high-stakes types.
After each traffic shift, monitor production metrics for 24 to 48 hours before proceeding to the next increment; define rollback triggers in advance.
Delete the legacy codebase and its infrastructure within one sprint of reaching zero traffic; deferred cleanup becomes permanent technical debt.
Examples
Routing facade with shadow mode
Feature-flag driven routing function

# routing_fn.py
# Routing decisions driven by feature flags. No deployment required to change routing.
# Integrate with LaunchDarkly, Unleash, or any flag SDK.

import hashlib
from routing_facade import Disposition, Request

# In production, these values come from your feature flag SDK:
# FLAGS = launchdarkly_client.variation(...)
# For illustration, we represent the flag state as a config dict.
FLAGS = {
    "new_agent_shadow_pct": 10,        # 10% of all traffic runs in shadow mode
    "new_agent_live_request_types": [  # request types fully migrated to new agent
        "summarize",
        "classify",
    ],
    "new_agent_live_cohorts": [        # user cohorts fully migrated
        "internal_employees",
        "beta_users",
    ],
}

def routing_fn(request: Request) -> Disposition:
    # Fully migrated request types always go to new agent
    if request.request_type in FLAGS["new_agent_live_request_types"]:
        return Disposition.NEW

    # Fully migrated cohorts always go to new agent
    if request.user_cohort in FLAGS["new_agent_live_cohorts"]:
        return Disposition.NEW

    # Deterministic shadow mode assignment (consistent per request_id)
    hash_val = int(hashlib.md5(request.request_id.encode()).hexdigest(), 16)
    if hash_val % 100 < FLAGS["new_agent_shadow_pct"]:
        return Disposition.SHADOW

    return Disposition.LEGACY
Real-world example

A B2B SaaS company migrated its document classification feature from a fine-tuned BERT model to a Claude-based agentic classifier that could also explain its classifications. The migration used a strangler pattern across eight weeks.

In weeks one and two, the routing facade was deployed with all traffic directed to the BERT model. The Claude agent ran in shadow mode on 10 percent of documents. Shadow comparisons revealed that the agent agreed with BERT on 94 percent of classifications; the 6 percent divergence was reviewed manually and found to be split equally between the agent being more accurate and the agent being less accurate on domain-specific vocabulary.

In weeks three through six, the team migrated classifications by document category, starting with the highest-confidence categories from the shadow analysis. Each category migration required eval suite passage and 24 hours of production metric stability. By week six, the agent handled 70 percent of live traffic. In weeks seven and eight, the remaining categories were migrated, the routing facade was simplified to a passthrough, and the BERT model infrastructure was decommissioned. Total migration occurred with zero production incidents and no user-visible regressions.
Common mistakes
Skipping shadow mode and going directly to canary

Shadow mode on 5 to 10 percent of traffic is cheap and reveals quality issues on real inputs with zero user impact. Teams that skip shadow mode and go directly to a 5 percent live canary expose real users to regressions that shadow mode would have caught. Always run shadow mode for at least 48 hours on a representative traffic sample before shifting any live traffic to the new agent.
Not defining rollback triggers in advance

A migration that does not define in advance the metric thresholds that trigger a rollback will have long, painful post-incident arguments about whether to roll back. Define rollback triggers before each increment: for example, 'if the new agent's error rate exceeds legacy error rate by more than 2 percentage points for 15 consecutive minutes, shift all traffic back to legacy automatically.' Automated rollback via feature flag update is strongly preferred over manual intervention.
Best practices

Instrument shadow mode comparisons with semantic similarity scoring (embedding cosine similarity or LLM-as-judge) rather than exact string matching, since the new agent will use different phrasing than the legacy system even when the content is equivalent.
Keep the routing facade stateless; any state required for routing decisions (cohort membership, request type) should live in the request object or in an external flag service, not in the facade.
Define a hard deadline for completing Phase 3 (legacy deletion) at the start of the migration; without a deadline the legacy system accumulates indefinitely.
Treat shadow mode divergence data as a training signal: the cases where the new agent and legacy disagree, reviewed by domain experts, are the highest-value additions to the eval suite.
Version the routing facade configuration separately from application code so traffic shifts can be deployed independently of feature code.
Performance and scalability

Shadow mode doubles the LLM API call volume and cost during the shadow phase. For high-traffic systems, limit shadow mode sampling to the minimum percentage needed for statistical confidence rather than running it on all traffic. At typical production traffic volumes, 5 to 10 percent sampling is sufficient to detect quality differences with statistical significance within 24 to 48 hours.

The routing facade itself adds one function call of latency per request — negligible relative to LLM call latency. Ensure the shadow mode thread pool is bounded to prevent resource exhaustion under traffic spikes; use a queue with a max size and drop shadow requests silently when the queue is full. Shadow drops should be monitored but do not require incident response.
Tooling and ecosystem

LaunchDarkly / Unleash / Flipt: feature flag platforms that provide percentage rollout, cohort targeting, and instant flag updates without code deployment — the natural mechanism for controlling the routing function.
Datadog / Prometheus: observability platforms for tracking the side-by-side latency, error rate, and cost metrics for legacy and new agent paths.
Weights and Biases / LangSmith: eval and tracing platforms for capturing and comparing shadow mode outputs at scale.

Security and reliability

Shadow mode requests are copies of production requests and may contain personally identifiable information (PII). The shadow mode path must apply the same data handling and retention policies as the live path. If the new agent logs shadow mode inputs for quality analysis, those logs must be subject to the same access controls and retention limits as production logs.

The routing facade is a new critical path component. Deploy it with the same reliability standards as the systems it fronts: health checks, graceful degradation (fall back to legacy if the facade itself fails), and circuit breakers on both downstream paths. A facade that fails closed (blocking all requests) is more dangerous than the legacy system it was protecting.
Key takeaways

The strangler pattern migrates AI features incrementally by routing traffic through a facade that dispatches to either the legacy system or the new agent, enabling controlled rollout and instant rollback.
Shadow mode — running the new agent on copies of production requests without surfacing outputs to users — is the safest and highest-value phase of the migration.
Each migration increment must pass the eval suite and meet predefined acceptance criteria before live traffic shifts to the new agent.
Define rollback triggers and automated rollback mechanisms before beginning any live traffic migration.
Shadow mode doubles LLM costs; limit sampling to the minimum percentage needed for statistical confidence rather than running it on all traffic.
Set a hard deadline for legacy deletion at the start of the migration; open-ended dual-system maintenance is expensive and demoralizing.
